title: Topic 379: Why Gaming Reviews Are Controversial: Subjectivity and Scores
content:
## Topic 379: Why Gaming Reviews Are Controversial: Subjectivity and Scores

### **ABSTRACT**

This paper bravely ventures into the digital Colosseum where video game reviews are thrown to the lions of public opinion. It examines the fundamentally absurd and endlessly entertaining conflict that arises from the attempt to assign a cold, hard number to the subjective, emotional rollercoaster of interactive entertainment. We posit that the controversy surrounding game reviews is not merely a matter of differing opinions, but a complex psychological phenomenon fueled by cognitive biases, the crushing weight of aggregate scores, and the bizarre reality that a 7/10 is often perceived as a catastrophic failure. Through a series of real-world case studies and a tongue-in-cheek application of psychological principles, this paper will demonstrate that the outrage over a video game score says less about the game itself and more about the beautifully irrational nature of the human mind. In short, we’re here to find out why so many people get so very angry about a number on a screen.

### **INTRODUCTION**

Picture the scene: a gamer, let’s call him Kevin, has just spent 80 hours of his life conquering the sprawling open world of *Dragon’s Odyssey 4*. He’s slain the final boss, shed a single, manly tear at the poignant ending, and is basking in the afterglow of a truly epic experience. He opens his favorite gaming website, eager to see the glowing praise that will validate his life choices for the past three weeks. And there it is. The review. The score: 7.8/10. The summary: “A solid, if uninspired, entry in the series. Too much water.” Kevin’s world shatters. This isn’t a review; it’s a personal attack. An affront to his very identity. How could they? After all he’s been through?

This little drama, played out daily across the internet, is the heart of our inquiry. We live in an age of quantification, where every movie, every restaurant, and every Uber driver is reduced to a star rating. Yet, nowhere does this practice produce more spectacular displays of outrage than in the world of video games. The attempt to marry the subjective artistry of a game with the objective finality of a numerical score is a shotgun wedding doomed to perpetual conflict. It’s like trying to give a numerical rating to your first kiss or grading a sunset on a curve. The very idea is comical, yet its consequences are taken with a deadly seriousness that borders on the absurd.

This paper will dissect this unholy matrimony of art and arithmetic. We will explore the psychological phenomena that turn a simple opinion into a declaration of war, from the cognitive biases that color our perceptions to the social dynamics that transform a comment section into a digital mob. We will examine the monolithic influence of aggregators like Metacritic, which have become the all-powerful arbiters of success and failure in the multi-billion dollar gaming industry. And we will delve into the real-world stories of reviews that became legendary controversies, not for their insight, but for the sheer scale of the internet’s reaction. Our thesis is simple: the controversy over game reviews is a beautiful, chaotic, and hilarious window into the quirks of human psychology and consumer behavior. So, grab your pitchforks and torches; it’s time to find out why a number can cause so much trouble.

### **LITERATURE REVIEW**

One might assume that the hallowed halls of academia would be teeming with scholarly works on the existential angst triggered by a 7/10 review for a beloved video game. One would be sadly mistaken. While the Nobel committee has yet to recognize "Excellence in the Study of Digital Entertainment-Induced Fury," a survey of adjacent fields in psychology and information science provides a surprisingly robust framework for understanding this phenomenon. The primary culprits, it appears, are the human brain’s endearing irrationality and the seductive, yet treacherous, allure of numbers.

The very concept of a numerical rating system is built on a foundation of psychological quicksand. Researchers have long noted that the act of assigning a number to a qualitative experience creates an illusion of objectivity that is both powerful and misleading [1]. This "tyranny of the average," as some might call it, is particularly potent in the digital age, where aggregator sites like Metacritic have become the de facto arbiters of quality. The weighted average, a seemingly rational process, is in fact a black box of subjective weightings and editorial decisions, yet it produces a single, monolithic number that is often treated as gospel [2].

This numerical obsession provides fertile ground for a veritable parade of cognitive biases. **Confirmation bias**, the tendency to favor information that confirms one's existing beliefs, is rampant in the world of game reviews. Gamers who have already decided they love (or hate) a game will actively seek out reviews that validate their opinion, while dismissing contradictory ones as the ravings of a clueless hack [3]. This is closely followed by the **anchoring bias**, where the first piece of information received—often the Metacritic score—disproportionately influences all subsequent judgments. A game that launches with a 93 Metascore is immediately perceived as a masterpiece, while one that debuts at 75 is forever tainted with the stench of mediocrity, regardless of the actual content of the reviews.

Furthermore, the social dynamics of online communities amplify these individual biases into a collective roar. The **bandwagon effect**, or "herd mentality," is a powerful force in online discourse. A few influential reviews can create a snowball of public opinion that becomes nearly impossible to stop, leading to the now-infamous phenomenon of "review bombing," where users coordinate to flood a game's page with negative ratings, often for reasons entirely unrelated to the game's quality [2]. Studies on social influence in online reviews have shown that the opinions of others, particularly when presented in aggregate form, can significantly sway individual judgment, creating a feedback loop of praise or scorn that can make or break a game’s reputation [4]. In essence, the controversy over game reviews is not just a disagreement between individuals; it is a battleground where the inherent flaws of human cognition clash with the unforgiving logic of the number.

### **ANALYSIS: THE PSYCHOLOGY OF THE SCORE**

At the core of the storm surrounding video game reviews lies a profound psychological discomfort known as **cognitive dissonance**. This is the mental stress experienced by an individual who holds two or more contradictory beliefs, ideas, or values at the same time. In our context, the dissonance arises when a gamer's deeply held belief ("*Dragon’s Odyssey 4* is a masterpiece and my 80 hours were well spent") clashes with a contradictory piece of information (a 7.8/10 review). The brain, in its desperate attempt to resolve this conflict, often chooses the path of least resistance: discredit the source of the new information. The reviewer is not just wrong; they are an idiot, a shill, or a troll. This is not a rational response; it is a defense mechanism against the terrifying possibility that one's own judgment is flawed, or worse, that one has wasted a significant chunk of one's life on a "mediocre" experience.

This psychological drama is played out on the bizarre stage of the 7-9 scoring scale. In the strange world of video game reviews, a 7/10 is not a good score. It is a mark of profound mediocrity, a polite way of saying the game is a failure. An 8 is considered "good," and a 9 is the minimum for a game to be considered truly great. The scores of 1 through 6 are reserved for games that are fundamentally broken or actively hostile to the player. This unwritten rule, universally understood by the gaming community, creates a compressed and highly volatile scoring environment. The difference between an 8.8 and a 9.0 is not 0.2 points; it is the chasm between "pretty good" and "all-time classic." This arbitrary inflation of scores means that even a slightly negative review can feel like a catastrophic indictment.

The reviewer, then, becomes the perfect scapegoat for the gamer's disappointment and the developer's frustration. They are the messenger who is shot, dismembered, and then shot again for good measure. The internet provides a perfect, anonymous forum for this projection of anger. A reviewer who dares to give a beloved game a less-than-perfect score will have their motives, their skill, their intelligence, and even their personal life called into question. They are not just a person with a different opinion; they are an enemy of the community, a saboteur who is actively trying to harm the game and its fans.

This pressure is not just felt by the reviewers. The developers themselves are often held hostage by the almighty Metascore. The most infamous example of this is the case of *Fallout: New Vegas*. The developer, Obsidian Entertainment, had a clause in their contract with the publisher, Bethesda Softworks, that they would receive a significant bonus if the game achieved a Metascore of 85 or higher. The game's final score was 84. One point. One single, solitary point on an arbitrary scale determined by a secret algorithm, and the creators of a critically and commercially successful game were denied their bonus [2]. This is the absurd reality of an industry that has allowed a single number to become the ultimate measure of a game's worth, a number that can have a very real and very significant impact on the lives and livelihoods of the people who create the games we love.

### **REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES: TALES FROM THE TRENCHES**

To truly appreciate the theater of the absurd that is video game review controversy, one must move from the theoretical to the anecdotal. The history of gaming is littered with cautionary tales and legendary incidents that serve as perfect illustrations of the psychological phenomena at play.

Perhaps the most notorious case is the **“Kane & Lynch: Dead Men” affair** of 2007. When Jeff Gerstmann, a respected editor at GameSpot, gave the mediocre shooter a lukewarm 6/10, he was promptly fired. The game’s publisher, Eidos, had been a major advertiser on the site, and the implication was clear: a negative review had cost the company advertising revenue, and Gerstmann had paid the price. The incident, which became known as “Gerstmanngate,” was a stark and sobering look at the immense financial pressures that can influence the supposedly objective world of game reviews [5].

Equally illustrative is the infamous **“Too Much Water” incident** involving IGN’s 2014 review of *Pokémon Omega Ruby* and *Alpha Sapphire*. The review was largely positive, but the final summary famously docked points for “too much water” in a game set in a tropical archipelago. The critique, while arguably valid from a gameplay perspective, became an instant and enduring meme, a shorthand for nitpicky, out-of-touch criticism. The community’s reaction was not one of anger, but of widespread mockery, a collective rolling of the eyes at a review that seemed to miss the point entirely [5].

Sometimes, the outrage is not about a negative review, but a review that is not positive enough. When IGN’s Lucy O’Brien gave the highly anticipated *Uncharted 4: A Thief’s End* a 9/10, calling it a “remarkable achievement,” she was met not with thanks, but with a torrent of abuse from fans who were incensed that the game had not received a perfect 10. The fact that a 9/10 could be considered an insult is a perfect demonstration of the inflated and irrational expectations that permeate the gaming community [5].

And then there is the case of Jim Sterling’s 7/10 review for *The Legend of Zelda: Breath of the Wild*. In a sea of perfect 10s, Sterling’s dissenting opinion was seen as an act of heresy. The review, which praised the game but also pointed out what Sterling saw as significant flaws, was enough to drop the game’s Metacritic score from a 98 to a 97. The result? Sterling’s website was hit with a DDoS attack, a digital punishment for the crime of having a different opinion [5]. This incident is a chilling example of the herd mentality in action, where any deviation from the accepted consensus is met with swift and aggressive retribution.

### **DISCUSSION: WHAT DOES IT ALL MEAN?**

The sound and fury surrounding video game reviews, while often comical, is not entirely frivolous. It offers a fascinating glimpse into the evolving relationship between consumers, creators, and the media in the digital age. The intensity of the reactions suggests that for many, a video game is not merely a product; it is an investment of time, money, and emotion. Consequently, a game review is not just a piece of consumer advice; it is a judgment on that investment. Our choice of games, and our opinions on them, become extensions of our identity. A negative review of a game we love can feel like a personal insult because, on some level, it is. It questions our taste, our judgment, and the value of our leisure time.

This sense of personal investment is amplified by the **Metacritic effect**. The aggregation of dozens of reviews into a single, seemingly authoritative number has had a profound and arguably detrimental impact on the industry. It has created a system where nuance is lost, and a game’s fate can be sealed by a single digit. This has led to a risk-averse culture among major publishers, who are incentivized to create games that are consistently “good” (i.e., likely to score in the mid-80s) rather than taking creative risks that might result in a more divisive, but potentially groundbreaking, game. The Metacritic score has become a self-fulfilling prophecy: a high score drives sales, and a low score can condemn a game to obscurity, regardless of its actual merits.

The relentless focus on a numerical score also threatens to devalue the art of criticism itself. Thoughtful, nuanced discussion about a game’s themes, mechanics, and artistic achievements is often drowned out by the clamor over the final number. Is a game a 7 or an 8? The answer to that question has become more important than the “why.” This is a loss for everyone: for gamers, who are deprived of insightful criticism that could deepen their appreciation of the medium; for critics, who are reduced to the role of score-dispensers; and for developers, whose work is judged not on its own terms, but by its position on an arbitrary numerical scale.

And yet, there is a strange, paradoxical upside to all this madness. The memes, the in-jokes, and the shared outrage that erupt from these controversies are a form of community-building. The “too much water” meme, for example, is a piece of cultural shorthand that instantly identifies one as a member of the gaming community. In a strange way, the collective absurdity of it all is a coping mechanism, a way for a passionate and often fragmented community to process the inherent contradictions of a system that tries to quantify fun. The humor is not just a byproduct of the controversy; it is an essential part of it.

### **CONCLUSION**

We have journeyed through the looking glass into a world where a 7 is a failing grade, a missing bonus can be traced to a single decimal point, and the phrase “too much water” can become a battle cry. The controversy surrounding video game reviews is, in the final analysis, a beautiful mess. It is the inevitable result of our very human attempt to impose cold, hard logic on the gloriously illogical experience of art. We have seen how cognitive biases turn us into unwitting soldiers in a war of opinions, how the social dynamics of the internet can amplify a single voice into a deafening roar, and how the weight of a single number can shape the creative landscape of an entire industry.

The fundamental conflict between subjective experience and objective measurement is, of course, unsolvable. There will never be a perfect algorithm for fun, no universally accepted metric for joy. And perhaps that is for the best. The endless debate, the passionate arguments, and the sheer, unadulterated absurdity of it all are, in their own way, a testament to the power of the medium. It shows that people care, deeply and passionately, about the games they play.

So, the next time you see a review that fills you with incandescent rage, take a moment. Take a deep breath. And remember that you are not just angry about a number. You are a participant in a grand, ongoing, and utterly hilarious psychological experiment. And in a world that often takes itself far too seriously, there is something to be said for that. After all, they’re just games. Right?
references:
[1] Bedeley, R. T., Hao, H., & Ghoshal, T. (2025). Cognitive Biases in Online Opinion Platforms: A Review and Mapping. *Sage Open*, *15*(1). https://doi.org/10.1177/21582440251315564

[2] Wikipedia. (2024). *Metacritic*. https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Metacritic

[3] Crist, C. (2021). *Cognitive Bias*. WebMD. https://www.webmd.com/mental-health/what-is-cognitive-bias

[4] Cheng, Y. H., & Ho, H. Y. (2015). Social influence's impact on reader perceptions of online reviews. *Journal of Business Research*, *68*(4), 883-887.

[5] Paradis, D. (n.d.). *Top 10 Most Controversial Video Game Reviews*. WatchMojo. https://www.watchmojo.com/articles/top-10-most-controversial-video-game-reviews
